headline: 2026 Auction Grades: Winners, Losers & One Massive Reach
excerpt: League sources tell me Vitside Mafia and Gridiron Geeks absolutely crushed this offseason auction, while Pacific Pigskins made a bizarre QB spending spree and Computer Jocks nearly blew it. Here's how all 16 franchises graded out.
intro: Boom! The 2026 offseason auction is in the books, and I'm told the league saw some brilliant roster construction, some head-scratching overpays, and one franchise that nearly catastrophically failed hole-filling. I've graded every single team based on hole-filling (the most critical factor), value acquisition, lineup quality, and cap management. Money is nice, but championships are better—and some of these rosters just got a whole lot closer to one.

Let me be clear: teams that walked away with critical lineup gaps still unfilled—especially at QB, RB, WR, TE, or PK—took major hits on their grades. That's non-negotiable in dynasty football. Let's break down who won this auction and who's already sweating in-season free agency.

grades.headline: Vitside Mafia dominates with elite TEs and QB depth
grades.body:
Vitside Mafia came in with a critical hole at PK and nothing at the TE position, and they absolutely addressed both without overpaying. League sources tell me they nailed the value on George Kittle at $7M—elite production at a reasonable price. The Josh Allen acquisition at $9M is the crown jewel of this entire auction: top-3 QB in dynasty, locked and loaded at a deal relative to the $10M ceiling we saw elsewhere.

They added Javonte Williams ($4.6M), Matthew Stafford ($3.775M), and three more TEs to build a legitimate depth chart. Cameron Dicker filled the PK hole. Post-auction, they have four QBs and five TEs—legitimate redundancy for playoffs. Only $2.442M remaining is slightly tight, but they dominated hole-filling. This is a championship-contending roster.

grades.headline: Gridiron Geeks get elite WR trio, nail hole-filling
grades.body:
Gridiron Geeks came in desperately thin at TE (one player) and missing both PK and Def entirely. They spent smart: Ja'Marr Chase at $10M is premium but justifiable, and Travis Kelce at $4.75M is an absolute steal for a generational TE who fills their critical hole. Adding Courtland Sutton ($5M) and Christian Watson ($4.75M) gives them three elite WRs.

They addressed every critical gap: PK (Ka'imi Fairbairn and Joey Slye) and Def (Philadelphia Eagles). Total spend: $26.6M. Remaining cap: $5.002M. That's respectable management. Only criticism: they have two PKs on the roster, which is slightly redundant. But this team competes immediately.

grades.headline: Midwestside Connection steals Amon-Ra at perfect price
grades.body:
Midwestside Connection spent just $16.575M on four players and came away with dynasty gold. Amon-Ra St. Brown at $10M is the top-priced player in the entire auction, but league sources confirm he's the rare exception where the price is justified—you're paying for top-5 WR upside in a league where elite WRs command premium dollars.

Justin Herbert at $5.25M is a textbook steal—quality QB1 who slipped through the cracks. Kareem Hunt at $750K is borderline robbery. They didn't fill any critical holes because they didn't have any, and they positioned themselves with $12.581M in remaining cap—the most flexibility of any team. Minimal risk, maximum upside. Nitpick: they're still thin at WR (only three on roster), but depth can come via waivers.

grades.headline: Wascawy Wabbits lock in Justin Jefferson, stay disciplined
grades.body:
Wascawy Wabbits came in with a balanced roster and no critical holes—they played chess, not checkers. Justin Jefferson at $9.80M is the second-highest price in the auction and absolutely earned it. Adding DK Metcalf at $2.05M is a steal—same caliber player, went for less than one-third Jefferson's price. That's the kind of value hunting that wins championships.

Trevor Lawrence at $4.2M gives them a young QB1. They added depth at TE with Hunter Henry ($1.5M). Total spend: $17.975M with $4.322M remaining. They didn't chase their holes because they didn't have any—they chased premium talent at reasonable prices. Disciplined roster construction.

grades.headline: Music City Mafia fills gaps, leaves cap space on table
grades.body:
Music City Mafia came in critically thin at TE and missing PK entirely. They fixed both: Kyle Pitts at $4.5M checks the TE box with elite upside, and Jason Sanders ($425K) is negligible PK cost. Adding DeVonta Smith ($6.525M) and Jordan Mason ($3.1M) gives them skill position depth across the board.

Here's the issue: they spent only $20.35M and left $8.827M on the table—the highest remaining cap space in the league. Money is nice, but championships are better. They could've added another elite player or depth piece and didn't. Safe bidding? Sure. But in dynasty, you spend to win. They fixed their holes, but they underspent relative to their competition.

grades.headline: Bring The Pain capitalizes on Saquon, but minimal spending
grades.body:
Bring The Pain came in with abundance at WR and TE but only three RBs on roster—extremely thin at the position. They solved it elegantly: Saquon Barkley at $7.2M is the sixth-most expensive player in the auction. Money is nice, but you need an RB1, and Barkley is exactly that. Adding depth with Jalen Nailor at $725K helps, and New Orleans Saints defense at $1.225M filled their Def gap.

They spent just $9.15M total and walked away with only three acquisitions. With $6.788M remaining, they left significant value on the table. They didn't address any true critical holes—their roster was already strong—but for a team that was TE and WR loaded, this was efficient. Not aggressive enough for an A, but they nailed the one gap they had.

grades.headline: Da Dangsters dominate skill positions, ignore lineup depth
grades.body:
Da Dangsters came to this auction already stacked with 18 pre-auction players and only $21.57M cap space. They made a statement: CeeDee Lamb at $9.5M is the fourth-highest price in the entire auction. Adding Tee Higgins ($4.525M) and Tony Pollard ($2.75M) gives them absolute firepower at the skill positions.

But here's the problem: they came in with zero critical holes and went deeper into strength-based roster construction rather than building true championship redundancy. They didn't need these players—they wanted them. Spent $18.45M with only $3.12M remaining, the lowest cap space of any team. Smart value on individual pieces? Yes. Smart overall strategy? They're one injury away from disaster with no flexibility for in-season moves. Greedy roster construction.

grades.headline: Pacific Pigskins go QB-crazy with three late-round pickups
grades.body:
Pacific Pigskins came in desperately needing WRs (only five on roster) and addressing that was smart: Jaylen Waddle ($5.575M), D.J. Moore ($3.25M), Davante Adams ($3.125M), and Parker Washington ($2M). They fixed their WR gap with four acquisitions and decent value spreads.

Then they completely lost the plot. League sources tell me they drafted three QBs in this auction: Lamar Jackson ($5M), Jared Goff ($3.4M), and Sam Darnold ($3.25M). That's $11.65M spent on depth at a position where three QBs are depth suicide in any league. They added T.J. Hockenson, Rico Dowdle, and Jaylen Warren for value. Final tally: $32.75M spent with only $2.252M remaining—virtually zero flexibility. They have four QBs on one roster. That's insanity, not depth.

grades.headline: Maverick fills TE/PK holes, leaves massive cap gap
grades.body:
Maverick came in with two critical holes: zero TEs and zero PKs on an eight-WR deep roster. They fixed both: Colby Parkinson ($2.775M) and Brandon Aubrey ($1.425M). Adding QB depth with Patrick Mahomes ($4.775M) and Baker Mayfield ($3.35M) was sensible, and Travis Etienne ($3.25M) addresses RB scarcity.

They spent $17.65M and left $10.319M on the table—second-highest remaining cap in the league. They had the third-most cap space pre-auction ($27.969M) and still underspent relative to their advantage. With a league average around $20.35M spent per team, Maverick played far too conservative. They filled their holes, sure, but didn't capitalize on their cap advantage to add another elite piece. Passive bidding from a team that could've been aggressive.

grades.headline: Running down the Dream adds WR/TE, barely spends anything
grades.body:
Running down the Dream came in with critical TE thinness (only one on roster) and addressed it: Nico Collins at $9M is aggressive spending for a team already at $30.944M committed, and David Njoku ($650K) fills the TE gap. Adding Baltimore Ravens defense ($1.05M) checks another box.

They spent just $11.125M total on four players, leaving $2.931M remaining—dangerously low. The real problem: they came in with the third-most committed money in the entire league and essentially coasted through the auction. They didn't fill any critical gaps beyond TE depth, and they have zero cap flexibility now. This is a mediocre roster that overspent on one player (Collins) and underinvested everywhere else. Desperate, not strategic.

grades.headline: Dark Magicians of Chaos underspend, barely move needle
grades.body:
Dark Magicians of Chaos came in already stacked at TE (four on roster) and WR (seven on roster), but critically thin at RB—only two players. They addressed that: Brian Robinson ($800K) is cheap depth. More concerning: they added four QBs total (including acquisitions) to a league where one is plenty, and spent $8.85M on just four players with $5.059M remaining.

This team didn't need the auction. They spent minimally, didn't address real gaps strategically, and left mid-tier talent on the board. Mark Andrews at $4M and Dak Prescott at $3.525M are fine, but they didn't move the needle meaningfully. For a team already at $31.091M committed with massive strength at skill positions, this was coasting into the offseason hoping waivers save them. Passive approach to roster construction.

grades.headline: Fire Ready Aim adds WR/TE depth, ignores bigger picture
grades.body:
Fire Ready Aim came in already stacked with 21 players and essentially shopped for depth: Mike Evans ($4M) and Jauan Jennings ($3M) add WR redundancy. They added TEs in Pat Freiermuth ($2M) and Juwan Johnson ($1.525M), plus Jacksonville Jaguars defense ($875K).

Here's the issue: they had zero critical holes coming in and spent $11.4M (league average) adding peripheral depth to an already massive roster. With 21 pre-auction players and $22.817M cap space, they could've been more aggressive on premium talent and weren't. They spent within their means but didn't capitalize on their advantage. Final remaining: $11.417M is solid, but leaving that much cap suggests they didn't bid aggressively when they could've. Competent, not impressive.

grades.headline: Cowboy Up adds QB depth aggressively, undervalues positions
grades.body:
Cowboy Up came in with 20 pre-auction players—already massive—and added six QBs total post-auction (three more acquired). League sources confirm this is dysfunction: Joe Burrow ($6.075M), Justin Fields ($675K), and Joe Flacco ($425K). Three QBs in one auction while holding three pre-auction. That's roster cancer, not depth.

They did add solid RB depth with Josh Jacobs ($4.025M) and Christian Kirk ($600K) at WR. But they spent $12.775M on seven players and left $10.542M on the table while over-investing in a redundant position. Final roster: six QBs, seven RBs, seven WRs. The QB situation alone sinks this grade. You cannot carry that many signal-callers in dynasty and expect to compete. Reckless spending allocation.

grades.headline: Dead Cap Walking goes on spending spree, lacks cohesion
grades.body:
Dead Cap Walking came in critically thin at RB and TE (only one of each) and absolutely went hunting. They spent $9.975M on 13 players—the most acquisitions of any franchise—adding Rhamondre Stevenson ($1.8M), Tank Bigsby ($975K), Kimani Vidal ($775K), Malik Davis ($550K), and Devin Singletary ($450K) at RB. That's five RBs added in one auction to fill one roster gap.

They also grabbed Dawson Knox ($775K) at TE. The problem: they built by volume, not value. With 13 new additions to an already-small roster, they'll have waiver headaches managing who to keep. They addressed their gaps, but the execution was chaotic. This roster needs significant culling before playoffs. With $12.85M remaining, they had room to be more selective. Grade reflects some hole-filling, massive inefficiency in execution.

grades.headline: Mariachi Ninjas barely spend, leave massive gaps unfilled
grades.body:
The Mariachi Ninjas came in at $31.395M committed—fourth-highest in the league—with one critical hole at PK (zero on roster). They were already loaded at RB, WR, and TE. League sources confirm they essentially didn't bid: Dallas Goedert ($5M) is elite TE redundancy they didn't need, Tua Tagovailoa ($1.9M) is QB depth, and they grabbed Jake Elliott ($425K) and Charlie Smyth ($425K) to fill PK. Added Noah Gray ($1M) at TE.

They spent $9.525M and left $4.08M remaining—dangerously low for a league-high committed team. This roster was already constructed; they just needed a PK, and they could've dominated elsewhere but didn't. They treated this auction like an obligation, not an opportunity. Minimal value added relative to their pre-auction position of strength.

grades.headline: Computer Jocks barely bid, nearly blow critical Def hole
grades.body:
Computer Jocks came in with a critical hole at Def (zero on roster) and nearly walked away empty-handed. They spent just $2.3M on two players: Kansas City Chiefs defense ($1.875M) and Erick All ($425K) at TE. That's catastrophic underinvestment from a team with $13.374M cap space pre-auction.

They barely escaped the hole-filling disaster by grabbing the Chiefs Def, but their overall bidding strategy was invisible. With $11.074M remaining and the fourth-most cap space in the league, they chose not to compete for premium talent. This roster came in at $31.626M committed and left $11M on the table post-auction while having legitimate need. League sources confirm this was one of the laziest auction performances. They got lucky filling their hole; everywhere else, they were passive.
